summary: Gastric HCl is secreted by parietal (oxyntic) cells, and pepsinogen is secreted by chief (peptic) cells.
Gastric HCl is secreted by chief cells, and pepsinogen is secreted by parietal cells.
Both HCl and pepsinogen are secreted by mucous neck cells.
The cephalic phase is vagally mediated and occurs before food reaches the stomach.
The cephalic phase occurs when food enters the duodenum.
The cephalic phase is hormonally mediated by gastrin from the antrum.
Bile is produced by the liver, stored in the gallbladder, and emulsifies dietary fats.
Bile is produced by the gallbladder and stored in the common bile duct.
Bile is produced by the pancreas and aids in protein digestion.
The MMC is the interdigestive motility pattern regulated by motilin that sweeps residual food from the small intestine.
The MMC occurs during feeding and is stimulated by cholecystokinin.
The MMC is the peristaltic pattern that propels food through the oesophagus.
Most nutrient absorption occurs in the jejunum, while bile salts and B12 are absorbed in the ileum.
Most nutrient absorption occurs in the duodenum, while bile salts are absorbed in the jejunum.
Bile salts and vitamin B12 are absorbed in the duodenum along with most nutrients.
Cholecystokinin stimulates gallbladder contraction and pancreatic enzyme secretion.
Cholecystokinin inhibits gallbladder contraction and stimulates gastric acid secretion.
Secretin stimulates gallbladder contraction and bile production.
Intrinsic factor is secreted by parietal cells and is essential for vitamin B12 absorption in the terminal ileum.
Intrinsic factor is secreted by chief cells and absorbs iron in the duodenum.
Intrinsic factor is secreted by goblet cells and protects the gastric mucosa.

overallFeedback.feedback: Review GI physiology and try again.
